docs.text:
Cut rhythm by video format

- Short/Reel: cut every 1-2 seconds on average.
- Long-form explainer: cut every 4-8 seconds.
- Vlog: looser rhythm, locked to natural action rather than a fixed timer.

Hook formula: Beauty / Fashion content

The final result (full look) shown in 1 second before unfolding the
step-by-step process.
Formula: Final result -> "Here's how" -> steps.

Hook formula: Comedy / Sketch content

The very first line or situation must already contain an incongruity or
comedic twist. No long setup.
Formula: Immediate absurd situation -> development -> punchline.

Hook formula: Finance / Business content

A shocking or counter-intuitive number as the opener ("I lost $10,000
learning this"). Concrete numbers and gains/losses hook harder than an
abstract concept.
Formula: Shocking number or result -> problem/context -> explanation/lesson.

Hook formula: Fitness / Sport content

Show the transformation or result (before/after, record broken) as the
opener.
Formula: Visual result -> "Here's the program" -> exercise details.

Hook formula: Cooking / Food content

Tight, appetizing shot of the finished dish (visual + food sound) before
showing the prep.
Formula: Finished dish close-up + appetizing sound -> "Here's how" ->
prep steps.

Hook formula: Gaming content

Show the session's most spectacular moment (clutch, fail, record) in the
first 2 seconds, then rewind to loop back — the beginning is the ending.
Formula: Highlight -> "How I got here" -> full gameplay.

Hook formula: general rule across all themes

The hook must land in the first 1-3 seconds. Its only job is stopping the
scroll. The hook's format changes by theme/niche, but the base structure
stays the same: Promise or Tension + quick proof it's worth staying for.

Cross-theme rule: avoid the 3 hook killers regardless of theme: (1) a
logo/animated intro longer than 1 second, (2) a generic greeting line
("Hey everyone, today..."), (3) too much context before reaching the topic.
The first word or first image must already be "the content", not the
wrapper.

Hook formula: Motivation / Personal development content

A statement that goes against common wisdom ("Stop trying to be productive
every single day"), capturing attention through friction with what the
viewer thinks they know.
Formula: Counter-intuitive statement -> justification -> actionable advice.

Hook formula: Review / Unboxing content

Give the verdict or score right at the start ("Is this $500 product worth
it? My answer in 60 seconds"), not at the end. This creates tension: the
viewer stays to understand the "why".
Formula: Teased verdict -> product context -> detailed justification.

Hook formula: Tech / Dev / AI content

Show the final result BEFORE explaining how you got there ("Here's what I
built in 10 minutes with AI" + show the app running immediately). Avoid
opening with "Hey, today I'm going to show you..." — it kills attention
instantly.
Formula: Shocking result -> "How I did it" -> tutorial.

Hook formula: Travel content

The most spectacular shot of the location (drone, landscape, unique moment)
in the very first second, no intro text.
Formula: Shocking shot -> location title on screen -> the travel story.

Hook formula: Tutorial / Educational content

State the problem the viewer has likely already experienced, in one
sentence ("If your Python code always crashes on this error, watch this").
The viewer must recognize themselves within 2 seconds.
Formula: Recognizable problem -> Promise of a quick solution -> content.

Hook formula: Vlog / Lifestyle content

Start in medias res, not with "today I'll show you my day". Preview a
strong or funny moment from the middle of the day, then rewind to the
start.
Formula: Shocking clip from mid-video -> "How we got here" -> chronological
walkthrough.

Hook psychology: A/B testing method for hooks

1. Record the underlying video footage only once.
2. Edit three different hooks, each from a different psychological category.
3. Post 24-48h apart, or on different platforms.
4. Compare retention curves — most platforms show exactly where people drop
   off; a weak hook produces a "cliff" in the first 2 seconds.
5. Never re-upload the exact same video file cross-platform: platforms
   detect duplicates and suppress reach. Re-filming the hook, even from a
   slightly different angle, counts as original content.

For manual tracking (no bulk export exists for short-form retention),
recommended columns: date, platform, hook ID, views, retention at 3s/15s/30s.
One cited creator spends 60-110 minutes a day on this.

Hook psychology, community debate: from "guru" hooks to human-sounding hooks

Multiple sources converge on one point: today's best hooks sound like
genuine, well-articulated human moments, not something overtly "marketed".
Informal test cited: if the hook sounds like a dinner-table conversation,
people lean in; the moment it sounds like an ad, they drop off.

Related: the right balance between over-polished and over-fake speech — a
precisely targeted vulnerability — is what breaks down strangers' distrust
online. But once a vulnerability pattern gets identified and copied by
everyone, the "performed" versions become noticeable: what lasts over time
are versions that resemble genuine spontaneous thought rather than
calculated vulnerability performance.

Hook psychology: 12-month A/B benchmark, engagement rate by hook type

Data from a test where only the hook changes, identical content, posted on
Instagram/X/Reddit:

- "I spent $X and learned Y": 8.4% (best score)
- "Unpopular opinion:": 7.1%
- "Nobody talks about...": 4.2%
- "Here is what worked / what did not": 3.9%
- "X mistakes I made...": 3.1%
- "The truth about...": 2.4%
- "How to...": 1.8% (lowest score, seen as dated in 2026 and associated with
  paid-course imagery)

Additional insight: replying to all first-hour comments doubled reach on
identical content — the hook is not the only lever, first-hour activity
matters just as much.

Hook psychology: 30-day tracking of 30 hook styles (~180 videos)

Data collected across combined TikTok/Reels/Shorts:

- Overall average across all categories: 4,700 views (median 3,100 views).
- Controversial Pattern Interrupt: 14,300 views average, 73% 3-second
  retention — best score, roughly 3x the average.
- Ultra-Specific Promise: 12,100 views average, 68% 3-second retention —
  roughly 2.5x the average.
- Generic question (e.g. "Are you struggling with X?"): only 2,400 views,
  18% 15-second retention — clearly below average.

Hook psychology: brand-level hook patterns (3 families)

- Duolingo: pattern interrupt — the mascot does something unexpected in the
  first 2 seconds.
- Apple: visual contrast — clean, minimal opening, the eye locks onto the
  product immediately.
- Ryanair: self-deprecating honesty — openly admits its flaws ("our flights
  are cheap, don't expect luxury").
- Starbucks: sensory association — warm, cozy atmosphere established before
  the logo even appears.
- Nike: immediate relatability — shows someone struggling, empathetic
  register rather than aspirational.
- Tesla: statistic interrupt — opens on a fact that challenges common
  thinking before showing the product.
- Red Bull: in medias res entry — no intro, viewer is dropped straight into
  extreme action.

These 7 patterns group into 3 psychological responses: pattern break
(Duolingo, Red Bull), emotional resonance (Starbucks, Nike, Ryanair), and
curiosity gap (Tesla, Apple).

Hook psychology, part 1: Curiosity Gap, Problem Amplification, Authority Challenge, Transformation

Curiosity Gap: the gap between what's known and what's wanted activates the
brain's dopamine reward-anticipation circuit. Weak if too vague ("I tried
something") — must be specific.

Problem Amplification: exploits loss aversion; avoided pain motivates more
than gained pleasure. Must name the precise symptom, not the general
category.

Authority Challenge: activates threat evaluation and belief updating;
conflict can't be ignored. Risky: without credible proof behind it, the
backlash is worse than not posting at all.

Transformation / Before-After: aspirational desire, same mechanism as
makeover shows. Works best with a concrete numeric contrast.

Hook psychology, part 2: Relatability, Urgency, Contradiction, Specificity Effect

Relatability / POV: social validation, confirms a shared experience.
Generates the most comments; validation test: "would someone tag a friend
under this?"

Urgency / FOMO: scarcity heuristic — what's limited or insider feels more
valuable. Manufactured urgency erodes trust over time.

Contradiction & Contrast: creates unresolved tension the brain can't let go
of without a response.

Specificity Effect: a bizarrely precise detail creates instant credibility
signal ("this person really lived this"). Must speak to one single person,
not an audience.

Hook psychology, part 3: Timeframe Tension, Honest Admission, Controversial Gotcha, Pattern Interrupt, Money-Specific

Timeframe Tension: short or unexpected deadlines trigger a curiosity loop
and hope of attainability. Present in almost every "growth story" hook.

Honest Admission / Vulnerability: counters "polished guru" fatigue,
self-deprecation becomes the hook. Must sound spontaneous, not rehearsed.

Controversial "Gotcha": a shocking claim followed by a hypothetical reveal.
Mixed community reception, close to ragebait — some dislike it while
admitting it works.

Pattern Interrupt (brand): an unexpected sound, visual, or claim breaks the
scroll before the message is even understood.

Money-Specific Hook: a precise dollar amount beats generic educational
framing, roughly 4x in one cited test.

Hook psychology: checklist of hook killers (eliminate systematically)

- Slow intro ("Hey guys, so today I wanted to talk about...").
- Payoff buried at the end of the video instead of teased upfront.
- First frame that looks like every other frame in the feed, nothing
  distinctive.
- No text overlay in the first second — many people watch muted.
- Manufactured urgency or fake scarcity.
- Authority challenge launched without credible proof behind it.
- Vulnerability that sounds "rehearsed" — audiences detect performed
  authenticity versus genuine spontaneous thought.

Hook psychology: content type -> recommended hook pattern mapping

- Tutorial / How-to -> Problem Amplification, Transformation. The viewer
  must feel the problem before wanting the solution.
- Product test -> Curiosity Gap, Urgency. Builds suspense around the
  verdict.
- Vlog / Day in the life -> Relatability, POV. Viewers stay for content
  reflecting their own experience.
- Hot take / Opinion -> Authority Challenge. Conflict draws attention.
- Storytime -> Curiosity Gap, Transformation. Sets up a narrative arc the
  viewer must finish.
- Limited offer / Launch -> Urgency, FOMO. Natural scarcity makes urgency
  authentic and credible.

Hook psychology, community debate: the hook explains the spike, not sustained growth

A viral hook can explain a one-off view spike without explaining an
account's long-term growth. Cited example: one account plateaued around 48k
followers after a viral hook, while another kept growing to about 98k. This
suggests the quality of content following the winning hook matters at least
as much as the hook itself for long-term growth.

Related: accounts whose topic is literally "how to grow on social media"
are among today's best-performing niches, often starting from zero and
exploding by teaching growth itself — a self-referential effect worth
remembering so as not to over-generalize their results to other niches.

Hook psychology, community debate: do these patterns only work for the growth/marketing niche?

Unresolved in the corpus. One argument holds these hooks only work because
growth/marketing audiences already want more views, biasing the
measurement. A counter-argument cites examples outside that niche (physical
therapy, language learning) with unverified numbers (roughly 40% of
high-retention Reels analyzed reportedly came from non-marketing accounts,
and changing the hook alone reportedly added +20-30% watch time).
Calibration note: this counter-argument remains anecdotal, not a hard
dataset — treat with caution.

Related debate: niche specificity beats generic pattern. A hook that works
in finance doesn't necessarily work in postpartum fitness. Test patterns
within your own niche rather than copying one that worked elsewhere
verbatim, even if the underlying psychological mechanism seems transferable.

Hook psychology: platform adaptation

- TikTok: raw, fast, chaotic energy. The hook must land on the very first
  word, no warm-up, with a mid-thought interruption effect. Bold text helps
  since many watch muted. Jump cuts in the first second signal dynamism to
  the algorithm.
- Instagram Reels: more polished, aspirational energy. About 0.5s more
  tolerance before a swipe. The first aesthetic frame matters more.
  Transformation and POV formats overperform, matching the platform's
  aspirational culture.
- YouTube Shorts: a slightly longer setup is tolerated; a two-sentence hook
  can work where TikTok demands one sentence. Retention is heavily rewarded
  by the algorithm. Curiosity gap and authority challenge work particularly
  well since the audience is used to more educational short-form content.

Hook psychology: quick self-administered tests before publishing

- Hook test: would the first second alone make someone stop scrolling? If
  not, the hook isn't finished.
- Relatability test: would someone tag a friend under this post? A "hmm
  interesting" reaction isn't enough — there must be a concrete urge to
  share.
- Thumb-stop rule: interesting visual + engaging music + a fast, clear hook
  together is what physically stops the scrolling thumb.

Hook psychology: source reliability notes

- Cited figures come from individual Reddit posts, unverified and not
  peer-reviewed: treat as practitioner heuristics, never as established
  statistical truths.
- A near-identical duplicate of the "1000+ viral hooks analyzed" post was
  identified (published twice, nearly identical text), as was a duplicate of
  the "47 hooks" guide reposted almost verbatim elsewhere — deduplicated in
  the dataset, mentioned here for transparency.
- Two source posts mentioned commercial tools (a "Free Viral Hook
  Generator", a dashboard named "Crescitaly") — excluded from the dataset as
  product placement in the source material, not genuine hook knowledge.

Hook psychology: the specificity rule (most repeated principle in the corpus)

The single most repeated principle across all analyzed discussions:

- Speak to one single person rather than an audience.
- "I tried something" (weak) vs "I tracked every dollar I spent for 30 days"
  (strong).
- "I spent $X and learned Y" beats a generic "How to..." framing by roughly
  4x in engagement rate.
- An "Ultra-Specific Promise" averages 12,100 views with 68% 3-second
  retention, versus 2,400 views and 18% 15-second retention for a generic
  question.

Operational rule: between a generic hook and a hyper-specific hook on the
same topic, always prefer the version containing a number, a sensory
detail, or an ultra-precise situation over a broad category.

Hook psychology: one-rule summary

A strong hook always combines four elements: a clear psychological
mechanism (curiosity, loss, authority, aspiration, belonging, or scarcity),
hyper-concrete specificity (a number, a sensory detail, a precise
situation), an adaptation to the platform's own rhythm, and a delivery that
sounds like a real human speaking rather than an ad.

SFX and visual references: Beauty / Fashion content

SFX: soft, satisfying sounds (product application, fabric); pop sound on
look transitions.
Refs: polished lighting (ring light style), before/after via split-screen or
morph transition, close-ups on texture and finish detail.

SFX and visual references: Comedy / Sketch content

SFX: exaggerated comedic sounds (record scratch, boing, buzzer), timed to
the exact frame of the punchline.
Refs: sharp, fast cuts on punchlines; sudden zoom to emphasize a reaction.

SFX and visual references: Finance / Business content

SFX: discreet, professional sounds (no comedic SFX); a light impact sound
when key numbers appear on screen.
Refs: charts/text on screen for data, stable and sober framing, a paced
rhythm that lets information sink in.

SFX and visual references: Fitness / Sport content

SFX: sound impact on key reps/efforts; energetic, rhythmic music running
continuously.
Refs: fast cuts locked to the music tempo, slow-motion on technical
movements, before/after split-screen.

SFX and visual references: Cooking / Food content

SFX: bring ASMR food sounds forward (sizzling, chopping, pouring); little to
no music layered over these moments.
Refs: macro close-ups on ingredients and the finished dish, warm lighting,
cuts synced to preparation gestures.

SFX and visual references: Gaming content

SFX: keep the game's own audio as the base; add impact/whoosh sounds only on
highlights (kill, win, fail).
Refs: fast cuts locked to the action, small webcam overlay, reactive
on-screen text (emotes, counters).

SFX and visual references: combining niches

If a video crosses two themes (e.g. a tech tutorial filmed vlog-style),
combine the recommendations of both categories rather than picking only
one — but always keep priority on the creator's usual personal style.

SFX and visual references: Motivation / Personal development content

SFX: marked silences after strong statements; a rising riser sound on
emotionally intense moments.
Refs: close-up face shots for intimacy, few cuts during key passages,
on-screen text quoting the strong statement.

SFX and visual references: Review / Unboxing content

SFX: a small pop/click when a score or verdict appears on screen; silence
right before revealing the final rating to build tension.
Refs: clean, well-lit product shots; split-screen for comparisons; on-screen
text for technical specs.

SFX and visual references: Tech / Dev / AI content

SFX: digital/synthetic sounds (short beeps, glitches, "typing" sound during
code moments); avoid overly organic sounds.
Refs: terminal aesthetic, dark mode, gradients, quick cuts on results,
screen-capture shots prioritized over face shots.

SFX and visual references: Travel content

SFX: prioritize the location's ambient sound (nature, city, market) over
music; whoosh sound on transitions between locations.
Refs: wide/drone shots opening each sequence, cuts paced to the journey,
warm and immersive color grading.

SFX and visual references: Tutorial / Educational content

SFX: a subtle pop on each new step/number shown on screen; silence during
important explanations.
Refs: on-screen text on every key point, stable shots, moderate pacing
(neither too slow nor a speedrun).

SFX and visual references: Vlog / Lifestyle content

SFX: keep the location's natural ambient sound; minimal SFX (soft
transitions only); continuous background music rather than punctual SFX.
Refs: handheld or lightly stabilized camera, cuts paced to real action,
little on-screen text.

Visual reference: action or demo moment

Draw from sports/gaming edits: quick cuts locked to the music beat, dynamic
zoom, slow-motion on the key instant (typically around 50% speed).

Visual identity consistency

Always pick references that stay consistent with the creator's previous
videos (same colors, same font, same average cut rhythm) rather than
switching style every video. Visual recognition is part of a creator's
personal brand.

Visual reference: call-to-action / ending

Draw from educational-creator style: calmer tone, stable framing, simple
on-screen text (subscribe, link in description).

Visual reference: video opening / hook

Draw from fast-hook formats (MrBeast-style, tech creators): a punchy line + a
quick cut under 2 seconds, no long intro. The reference must match the
creator's own tone, not just copy whatever style is trending.

Visual reference: explanatory / storytelling section

Draw from documentary or narrative-vlog formats: slower shots, gentle camera
movement, on-screen text used to reinforce key points without cluttering the
frame.
